meta.setting: cold creek ford on a gravel bar below wooded Jefferson hills
meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in crossing risk and track reading

research_grounding.title: NPS The Wintu
research_grounding.use: Respectful regional grounding for Native presence around waterways.

research_grounding.title: California Department of Fish and Wildlife Deer

image_prompt.subject: a wagon team testing a creek ford while a Native family waits upstream, deer tracks mark the gravel, and a rider studies washed-out wheel ruts
image_prompt.scene: cold morning water over rounded stones; horses brace against current, a trout flashes near the shallows, no readable signs or labels
image_prompt.composition: square water-crossing scene with creek cutting horizontally through the frame and both banks holding different choices
image_prompt.camera: low bank-side view near the waterline, close to wheel ruts and hoof splashes
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Make the creek look like it knows more than any rider crossing it.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine ford crossing scene
render_traits: slightly simplified wagon team, riders, Native family, deer tracks, and creek stones
modest texture resolution on water, wet wood, harness, cloth, mud, and gravel
hard ambient occlusion under wagon wheels, hooves, bank brush, and stones
cold morning light with water reflections kept readable
believable 1899 clothing, tack, wagon gear, and regional riverbank detail
crossing-choice composition that feels playable without copying any known game scene
style_modifiers.palette: cold creek blue, gravel gray, wet wood brown, harness black, wool red-brown, deer-track mud, pine green, and pale morning sky.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable signs, wagon labels, marks, paper, or text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos
caricatured Native clothing or invented symbols

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The creek ford, wagon team, Native family, deer tracks, washed ruts, and water depth are readable.
Every visible object belongs plausibly to rugged 1899 river-crossing life.
The image treats Native people respectfully as real travelers with practical knowledge.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
